name: Thule
description: Thule, in classical and medieval literature, is a mythical island or landmass located far to the north, often associated with the Arctic regions. Its earliest mention comes from the Greek explorer Pytheas of Massalia in the 4th century BCE, who claimed to have reached it during his voyages. Accounts of Thule depict it as a land of extremes – perpetual daylight in summer, endless darkness in winter, and a landscape characterized by frozen seas and strange, unfamiliar phenomena. The precise location of Thule has always been debated; some scholars believe it refers to Iceland, Greenland, the Shetland Islands, or even Scandinavia. Over time, Thule has evolved from a geographical location into a symbol of the remote, the unknown, and the unattainable. It represents the edge of the known world, a place where the boundaries between reality and myth blur. In modern usage, "Ultima Thule" often symbolizes the ultimate goal or furthest possible achievement.
subtitle: A mythical island in the far north.
location: Far Northern Regions; Uncertain, possibly Iceland, Greenland, or Scandinavia.
inhabitants: Unknown, possibly mythical beings or hardy northern peoples
significance: Represents the edge of the known world and the allure of the unknown.

keyMyths.title: Pytheas's Voyage
keyMyths.description: Pytheas of Massalia claimed to have sailed to Thule in the 4th century BCE, describing it as a land of six months of daylight and six months of darkness. His account, though disputed by some contemporaries and later scholars, established Thule as a place of extreme northern latitude and unusual natural phenomena. The exact details of his voyage and the accuracy of his observations remain a subject of scholarly debate.
keyMyths.source: Pytheas of Massalia, On the Ocean

keyMyths.title: Thule as Ultima Thule
keyMyths.description: In later literature, Thule became known as 'Ultima Thule,' the furthest possible place, the ultimate limit of exploration and human experience. This concept fueled the imagination of writers and explorers for centuries, representing the pursuit of the unattainable and the desire to push the boundaries of the known world. Ultima Thule became a metaphor for the ideal.

keyMyths.title: Thule and the Hyperboreans
keyMyths.description: Connections have been drawn between Thule and the mythical land of the Hyperboreans, a people said to live beyond the North Wind in a state of perpetual happiness and blessedness. While not always explicitly linked, both Thule and the land of the Hyperboreans represent realms located at the edges of the world, characterized by unique and extraordinary conditions, prompting speculation about their relationship and cultural significance.

sources.author: Cary, M.
sources.work: The Geographic Background of Greek & Roman Literature
sources.description: Provides context for understanding ancient geographical knowledge and the perception of remote regions like Thule.
sources.text: Provides context for understanding ancient geographical knowledge and the perception of remote regions like Thule.

sources.author: Roller, Duane W.
sources.work: Pytheas of Massalia: Text, Translation and Commentary
sources.description: A comprehensive analysis of Pytheas's account of his voyage, including the descriptions of Thule.
sources.text: A comprehensive analysis of Pytheas's account of his voyage, including the descriptions of Thule.

sources.author: Stefánsson, Vilhjálmur
sources.work: Ultima Thule: Further Mysteries of the Arctic
sources.description: Explores the historical and cultural significance of Thule as a symbol of the Arctic and the unknown.
sources.text: Explores the historical and cultural significance of Thule as a symbol of the Arctic and the unknown.
